summary: Gruber reviews Microsoft's new Zune lineup announced in October 2007, comparing them unfavorably to Apple's iPod range. The new Zunes match iPod pricing and add Wi-Fi syncing, but offer nothing to compete with the flagship 160 GB Classic or iPod Touch. He notes that Microsoft's decision to announce via embargoed press briefing rather than a splashy live event signals low confidence. The post closes with a damning update: even DRM-free songs get wrapped with DRM restrictions when shared between Zunes, undermining their one potentially differentiating feature.
key_points: The new Zunes match iPod prices GB-for-GB with larger screens and Wi-Fi sync, but have no answer to the 160 GB Classic or iPod Touch
Wi-Fi syncing sounds appealing in concept but Microsoft's track record with Wi-Fi sharing on the original Zune gives reason for skepticism
Microsoft's subdued, embargoed announcement strategy undermines any attempt to compete with Apple's event-driven press coverage
Keeping Zune Windows-only hurts only Microsoft, since Apple sells iPods to both Mac and Windows users
DRM-free songs purchased or ripped still get wrapped with sharing restrictions when sent between Zunes, negating a potentially compelling differentiator
money_quotes: Wi-Fi synching is cool, in concept, but the devil is in the details — Wi-Fi song-sharing between devices is a cool idea, too, and we all know how well that feature worked out for Microsoft last year.
It's hard not to think that these are more or less exactly what Apple expected the new Zunes to be.
If you want to compete with the iPod, you've got to compete on all fronts, including press coverage.
In the past, notoriously, Microsoft has made new products Windows-only as a competitive bludgeon, but in the case of the Zune, who is hurt by keeping them Windows-only? Just Microsoft.
Apple can sell iPods to anyone using a Mac or Windows; Microsoft can sell Zunes only to Windows users.
DRM-free songs, including those you ripped yourself, will be wrapped with DRM when shared.
key_insight: The new Zunes represent Microsoft playing catch-up on specs while failing to compete on the things that actually matter — ecosystem, software experience, press strategy, and platform openness.
